narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application as a story of your journey toward becoming a lawyer, piecing together the chapters from your academic record, test scores, and background to predict how you'd fare on the bar exam. Your story starts strong in Year 1, landing you in the 5th decile at school, but by Year 3, that slipped to the 2nd decile, and those dips became the biggest signals whispering concerns about your consistency under pressure—the model's strongest reasons for seeing a risk of struggle ahead. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 28, which didn't quite stand out in the crowd of high achievers the model associates with bar success, pulling the outlook even further toward potential failure. Your upper-middle family income, while supportive, fit a pattern the model links to added hurdles in this high-stakes path, much like your undergraduate GPA of 3.3, which hovered solidly but not exceptionally. Even your background as a white male nudged things slightly in a positive direction, as did your choice to go full-time, yet those gentle boosts couldn't outweigh the heavier weights from your grades and LSAT. Together, these elements painted a picture where the model calculated a 44.8% chance of not passing the bar, a threshold that led to the tough call to reject your application. It's not a judgment on your potential or drive—many with similar starts have rewritten their stories through targeted effort. Perhaps focusing on bolstering those later-year grades or retaking the LSAT could shift the narrative next time. You've got the full-time commitment that hints at dedication; lean into that. This prediction is just one lens, not the final word on your capabilities. Keep pushing forward—law needs resilient voices like yours. What chapter will you write next?
